content:
# Genesis Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7v-8r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_013_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_013_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 8-9 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0012

---

*Abram Returns from Egypt (vv. 1–4)*

**13:1** And Abram went up [וַיַּעַל אַבְרָם] from Egypt [מִמִּצְרַיִם] he and his wife [הוּא וְאִשְׁתּוֹ] and all that was his [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] and Lot with him [וְלוֹט עִמּוֹ] toward the Negev [הַנֶּגְבָּה].

**13:2** And Abram was very heavy [וְאַבְרָם כָּבֵד מְאֹד] in livestock [בַּמִּקְנֶה] in silver [בַּכֶּסֶף] and in gold [וּבַזָּהָב].

**13:3** And he went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] on his journeys [לְמַסָּעָיו] from the Negev [מִנֶּגֶב] and as far as Bethel [וְעַד-בֵּית-אֵל] to the place [אֶל-הַמָּקוֹם] where his tent had been [אֲשֶׁר-הָיָה שָׁם אָהֳלֹה] in the beginning [בַּתְּחִלָּה] between Bethel and Ai [בֵּין בֵּית-אֵל וּבֵין הָעָי].

**13:4** To the place of the altar [אֶל-מְקוֹם הַמִּזְבֵּחַ] that he had made there [אֲשֶׁר-עָשָׂה שָׁם] at the first [בָּרִאשֹׁנָה] and Abram called there [וַיִּקְרָא שָׁם אַבְרָם] on the name of the LORD [בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה].

---

*The Strife Between Herdsmen (vv. 5–7)*

**13:5** And also to Lot [וְגַם-לְלוֹט] who was going with Abram [הַהֹלֵךְ אֶת-אַבְרָם] there was [הָיָה] flocks [צֹאן] and cattle [וּבָקָר] and tents [וְאֹהָלִים].

**13:6** And the land could not bear them [וְלֹא-נָשָׂא אֹתָם הָאָרֶץ] to dwell together [לָשֶׁבֶת יַחְדָּו] for their possessions were great [כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב] and they were not able [וְלֹא יָכְלוּ] to dwell together [לָשֶׁבֶת יַחְדָּו].

**13:7** And there was a strife [וַיְהִי-רִיב] between the herdsmen of Abram's livestock [בֵּין רֹעֵי מִקְנֵה-אַבְרָם] and between the herdsmen of Lot's livestock [וּבֵין רֹעֵי מִקְנֵה-לוֹט]; and the Canaanite [וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי] and the Perizzite [וְהַפְּרִזִּי] were dwelling then in the land [אָז יֹשֵׁב בָּאָרֶץ].

---

*Abram's Offer and Lot's Choice (vv. 8–13)*

**13:8** And Abram said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָם] to Lot [אֶל-לוֹט]: Let there be no strife, please [אַל-נָא תְהִי מְרִיבָה] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶיךָ] and between my herdsmen and between your herdsmen [וּבֵין רֹעַי וּבֵין רֹעֶיךָ] for we are men brothers [כִּי-אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים אֲנָחְנוּ].

**13:9** Is not the whole land before you [הֲלֹא כָל-הָאָרֶץ לְפָנֶיךָ]? Separate yourself, please [הִפָּרֶד נָא] from beside me [מֵעָלָי]: if to the left [אִם-הַשְּׂמֹאל] then I will go right [וְאֵימִנָה] and if to the right [וְאִם-הַיָּמִין] then I will go left [וְאַשְׂמְאִילָה].

**13:10** And Lot lifted up his eyes [וַיִּשָּׂא-לוֹט אֶת-עֵינָיו] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] the whole plain of the Jordan [אֶת-כָּל-כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן] that it was all well-watered [כִּי כֻלָּהּ מַשְׁקֶה] before the LORD destroyed [לִפְנֵי שַׁחֵת יְהוָה] Sodom and Gomorrah [אֶת-סְדֹם וְאֶת-עֲמֹרָה] like the garden of the LORD [כְּגַן-יְהוָה] like the land of Egypt [כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם] as you come to Zoar [בֹּאֲכָה צֹעַר].

**13:11** And Lot chose for himself [וַיִּבְחַר-לוֹ לוֹט] the whole plain of the Jordan [אֵת כָּל-כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן] and Lot set out toward the east [וַיִּסַּע לוֹט מִקֶּדֶם] and they parted [וַיִּפָּרְדוּ] each man from beside his brother [אִישׁ מֵעַל אָחִיו].

**13:12** Abram dwelt [אַבְרָם יָשַׁב] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] and Lot dwelt [וְלוֹט יָשַׁב] in the cities of the plain [בְּעָרֵי הַכִּכָּר] and he pitched his tent [וַיֶּאֱהַל] as far as Sodom [עַד-סְדֹם].

**13:13** And the men of Sodom [וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם] were wicked [רָעִים] and sinful against the LORD [וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה] exceedingly [מְאֹד].

---

*The LORD's Renewed Promise to Abram (vv. 14–17)*

**13:14** And the LORD said [וַיהוָה אָמַר] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם] after Lot had parted from him [אַחֲרֵי הִפָּרֶד-לוֹט מֵעִמּוֹ]: Lift up your eyes [שָׂא עֵינֶיךָ] and see [וּרְאֵה] from the place where you are [מִן-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה שָׁם] northward [צָפֹנָה] and southward [וְנֶגְבָּה] and eastward [וְקֵדְמָה] and westward [וְיָמָּה].

**13:15** For all the land [כִּי אֶת-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ] that you see [אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה רֹאֶה] to you I will give it [לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה] and to your seed [וּלְזַרְעֲךָ] forever [עַד-עוֹלָם].

**13:16** And I will make your seed [וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ] like the dust of the earth [כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ] so that if a man is able [אֲשֶׁר אִם-יוּכַל אִישׁ] to count [לִמְנוֹת] the dust of the earth [אֶת-עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ] also your seed shall be counted [גַּם-זַרְעֲךָ יִמָּנֶה].

**13:17** Rise up [קוּם] walk about in the land [הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ] through its length [לְאָרְכָּהּ] and through its breadth [וּלְרָחְבָּהּ] for to you I will give it [כִּי לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה].

---

*Abram Settles at Hebron (v. 18)*

**13:18** And Abram pitched his tent [וַיֶּאֱהַל אַבְרָם] and came and dwelt [וַיָּבֹא וַיֵּשֶׁב] by the oaks of Mamre [בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא] which are at Hebron [אֲשֶׁר בְּחֶבְרוֹן] and he built there [וַיִּבֶן-שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] to the LORD [לַיהוָה].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 13:1 — כָּבֵד: "Heavy" as the Image of Wealth

**וְאַבְרָם כָּבֵד מְאֹד בַּמִּקְנֶה בַּכֶּסֶף וּבַזָּהָב** — "And Abram was very heavy in livestock, in silver, and in gold."

The verb כָּבֵד (kaved, "to be heavy") gives its adjective כָּבֵד ("heavy") as the Hebrew idiom for wealth and abundance. This is the same root used for the "heaviness" of the famine that drove Abram to Egypt (12:10: כִּי-כָבֵד הָרָעָב, "for the famine was heavy"). The irony is exact and deliberate: the **heavy** famine drives Abram down to Egypt; he returns **heavy** with wealth. YHWH transforms the weight of scarcity into the weight of abundance. The EMET preserves "heavy" in the famine verse (12:10) and renders "heavy" here in v. 2 as an adjectival construction — "was very heavy" — to keep the lexical connection visible in English.

The wealth list — livestock, silver, gold — is deliberately ordered: livestock (the agrarian/pastoral base) precedes precious metals (the commercial base). Abram is both patriarch-herdsman and wealthy merchant. This combined profile sets up the conflict with Lot (v. 6: "their possessions were great").

**Contrast with the Negev journey (12:9 → 13:1-4):** Abram descends to Egypt via the Negev (12:9-10) and returns by the same route (13:1-3), retracing his steps to Bethel — to the altar, to the name of the LORD. The geographical return mirrors a spiritual return: the Egypt episode is a deviation (famine, deception, expulsion); the return to Bethel and Mamre is a return to covenant ground.

---

### On 13:3-4 — לְמַסָּעָיו: "On His Journeys"

**וַיֵּלֶךְ לְמַסָּעָיו** — "And he went on his journeys."

**מַסָּע (massa')** is the technical term for a stage or stopping-point in a journey — used later for the Israelite wilderness stations in Numbers 33 (where each encampment is listed as a מַסָּע). Abram retraces his earlier encampments, stage by stage, until he arrives at the original altar site between Bethel and Ai. The backward itinerary is not merely geographic: it is a narrative return to the moment before the Egyptian episode — Abram restoring himself, step by step, to the place and practice of covenant worship.

**בָּרִאשֹׁנָה / בַּתְּחִלָּה:** v. 3 uses **בַּתְּחִלָּה** ("in the beginning") for the location of his tent, and v. 4 uses **בָּרִאשֹׁנָה** ("at the first") for the altar. Both words denote "first/beginning" — the narrator piles up "first/beginning" language to emphasize that Abram is recovering his origins: the tent, the altar, the calling on the name. He is not building a new life after Egypt; he is returning to the life he had before Egypt.

---

### On 13:7 — אָז יֹשֵׁב בָּאָרֶץ: The Canaanite and the Perizzite

**וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי אָז יֹשֵׁב בָּאָרֶץ** — "and the Canaanite and the Perizzite were dwelling then in the land."

This aside parallels 12:6 ("and the Canaanite was then in the land") with a significant expansion: here both the **Canaanite** and the **Perizzite** are named. The Perizzite appears in no ethnic table (they are not in the Table of Nations, Genesis 10) and their origin is debated; they seem to be a non-urban or rural population of Canaan (possibly related to פְּרָזִי, "open-country dweller"). Their inclusion here may reflect the thoroughness of occupation — both the major coastal-urban Canaanite presence and the rural Perizzite population are in the land.

The aside functions as before (12:6): it contextualizes the conflict between Abram's and Lot's herdsmen against the background of a land already occupied. The subtext: the land promised to Abram's seed is not empty. The strife between the herdsmen of two Hebrew kinsmen is already occurring in the sight of Canaanite and Perizzite eyes. The theological weight: YHWH will keep his promise precisely in a contested land.

**אָז יֹשֵׁב** uses the same temporal marker אָז ("then") as 12:6. As before, the EMET preserves "then" without resolution of its temporal reference — whether the narrator writes from a post-conquest vantage point or simply marks historical contemporaneity remains an open question (see note on 12:6).

---

### On 13:8 — אַחִים: "Brothers"

**כִּי-אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים אֲנָחְנוּ** — "for we are men brothers."

**אַחִים (akhim, "brothers")** is here used in the broad sense of kinsmen or relatives, since Lot is Abram's nephew (בֶּן-אָחִיו, "son of his brother," 12:5). The LXX renders this ἄνθρωποι ἀδελφοί ἐσμεν ἡμεῖς ("we are men [who are] brothers"), agreeing closely with the MT. The appeal to kinship as the ground for non-strife is notable: Abram does not invoke hierarchy (he is senior and could simply command a separation) but brotherhood. He frames the potential conflict as a family problem requiring a gracious family solution.

The phrase **אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים** (men, brothers) is unusual — "men" and "brothers" in apposition. The word **אֲנָשִׁים** ("men") may carry a connotation of standing, honor, dignity — "we are men of standing, kinsmen." Abram's appeal is to the dignity of their shared family bond.

---

### On 13:9 — הִפָּרֶד נָא: The Grammar of Generosity

**הִפָּרֶד נָא מֵעָלָי** — "Separate yourself, please, from beside me."

The Niphal imperative **הִפָּרֶד** ("separate yourself") places the action grammatically in Lot's domain: Abram does not separate Lot — he asks Lot to separate *himself*. The polite particle נָא ("please/I pray") softens what could be a command into a request. Abram does not compel; he offers.

**The right-left formulation of v. 9** is remarkable in its generosity: Abram offers Lot first choice of the entire visible land, orienting the choice from Lot's perspective ("if to the left... if to the right"). This is an act of extraordinary grace — Abram, as the senior patriarch, holds prior claim to the land of divine promise, yet he defers to his nephew. The theological contrast is implied: Abram holds the land by promise, not by sight; he can afford to be generous because his security rests not on the visual quality of territory but on the word of YHWH.

---

### On 13:10 — The Double Simile: Garden of the LORD + Land of Egypt

**כְּגַן-יְהוָה כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם** — "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt."

This double simile is the literary and theological crux of Genesis 13. Two comparisons are placed in parallel to describe the Jordan plain before the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah:

1. **כְּגַן-יְהוָה** — "like the garden of the LORD": an allusion to the Garden of Eden (גַּן-עֵדֶן, 2:8-15). The Jordan plain resembles paradise — Eden-like abundance, irrigated terrain, lush vegetation.

2. **כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם** — "like the land of Egypt": the Nile valley, the paradigmatic well-watered fertile land, contrasted in Hebrew thought with rain-dependent Canaan (Deut. 11:10-11: "For the land that you are entering to possess is not like the land of Egypt... where you sowed your seed and watered it by foot like a vegetable garden. But the land you are crossing to possess is a land of hills and valleys, drinking water from the rain of heaven").

**The pairing of Eden and Egypt is extraordinary.** The Jordan plain is compared simultaneously to the primal garden of God and to the most famously fertile land on earth. These two archetypes of abundance bracket the highest and lowest registers of the biblical imagination:
- The garden of the LORD: the pre-fall world, paradise, divine ideal
- The land of Egypt: the well-watered, grain-rich, humanly productive world of civilization and empire

The double simile places Lot's choice of the Jordan plain at the intersection of these two models: he is choosing something that looks like paradise and works like Egypt. Both comparisons are, by the time of the narrator's writing, deeply ironic:
- The garden of Eden was lost through a choice made by sight (Gen. 3:6: "the woman saw that the tree was good for food and a delight to the eyes") — Lot looks, sees, and chooses exactly as Eve did.
- Egypt is the land of bondage: attractive, fertile, but the place from which YHWH must rescue his people. Abram himself has just returned from Egypt having been expelled; Lot is choosing a land that resembles the very place Abram was driven out of.

**The proleptic parenthesis**: "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah" (לִפְנֵי שַׁחֵת יְהוָה אֶת-סְדֹם וְאֶת-עֲמֹרָה) — the narrator inserts this devastating note into what should be Lot's moment of triumphant visual assessment. Even as Lot's eyes behold the beauty of the plain, the narrator has already told the reader it will be destroyed. Lot chooses the best-looking land in the region, and the reader already knows it will become a wasteland. The irony is total.

**כְּגַן-יְהוָה: The divine genitive.** "The garden of the LORD" (not merely "a garden" or "a paradise") elevates this comparison to the highest possible register. No human garden is the referent; it is YHWH's own garden — Eden — that the plain resembles. The comparison creates an implicit question: if Eden was lost through a wrong choice made "by sight," what does it mean that Lot also chooses "by sight" a land that looks like Eden?

**LXX 13:10:** ὡς παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ὡς ἡ γῆ Αἰγύπτου — "like the paradise of God and like the land of Egypt." The LXX renders כְּגַן-יְהוָה as παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ ("paradise of God") — the word **παράδεισος** (paradeisos) is the LXX's standard translation of גַּן (garden), here specifically Eden. This is the word that enters Christian vocabulary as "paradise." The LXX agrees with the MT in the double comparison; both comparisons appear in the same order. No textual variant.

---

### On 13:11 — מִקֶּדֶם: "Toward the East"

**וַיִּסַּע לוֹט מִקֶּדֶם** — "and Lot set out toward the east."

The directional phrase מִקֶּדֶם ("from/toward the east") is grammatically ambiguous — it can mean either "toward the east" (Lot moving east) or "from the east" (from the eastern side). Most interpreters read it as "toward the east," since Lot is moving from the central highlands (Bethel area) toward the Jordan plain which lies to the east and southeast.

But **eastward movement in Genesis carries heavy symbolic weight**: it is the direction of exile and separation from divine presence:
- Cain, cursed, "went away from the presence of the LORD and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden" (4:16)
- The Babel builders traveled "from the east" (11:2) — moving toward their pride-project
- Lot "moved toward the east" — away from Abram, toward Sodom

The eastward movement maps Lot's trajectory as a departure from the covenant center. Abram remains in the land of Canaan (v. 12: אַבְרָם יָשַׁב בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן) while Lot drifts east, progressively closer to Sodom — first in the cities of the plain, then "as far as Sodom" (v. 12), then "in Sodom" (14:12), then "sitting in the gate of Sodom" (19:1, the seat of civic authority).

---

### On 13:12 — וַיֶּאֱהַל עַד-סְדֹם: The Progressive Approach to Sodom

**וְלוֹט יָשַׁב בְּעָרֵי הַכִּכָּר וַיֶּאֱהַל עַד-סְדֹם** — "and Lot dwelt in the cities of the plain and he pitched his tent as far as Sodom."

The verb **וַיֶּאֱהַל** ("he pitched his tent") uses the same root (אָהַל) applied to Abram's tent at Bethel (12:8; 13:18). The comparison is deliberate: Abram pitches his tent at sacred sites (Bethel, Mamre/Hebron) at altars; Lot pitches his tent "as far as Sodom" — at the threshold of the city identified in the very next verse as the epitome of wickedness.

**עַד-סְדֹם ("as far as Sodom"):** The preposition עַד indicates approach, proximity, or boundary. Lot does not yet live *in* Sodom (that comes in Genesis 14:12); he camps at its edge. The narrative places him in a position of dangerous proximity — living close enough to be implicated (14:12: he is captured in the war of the kings precisely because he "was dwelling in Sodom"), yet not yet fully inside. The phrase captures the incremental quality of moral compromise: not a single leap into Sodom, but a progressive drawing near.

---

### On 13:13 — רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד: The Double Characterization of Sodom

**וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד** — "And the men of Sodom were wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly."

This is the narrator's editorial aside — a stark, unambiguous theological judgment inserted immediately after Lot chooses to settle near Sodom. The verse functions as a warning label placed over Lot's choice: *here is what he is moving toward*.

**Two Hebrew terms for moral failure:**

1. **רָעִים (ra'im, "wicked/evil"):** from the root רַע (ra', "evil/bad"). This is the general term for moral badness — the word used for the "evil" that YHWH saw in humanity before the flood (6:5: "the wickedness of man was great"). It is non-relational: mere ethical badness in the abstract.

2. **חַטָּאִים (hatta'im, "sinful/sinners"):** from the root חָטָא (khata', "to sin, miss the mark"). This term is relational — sin is defined relative to a standard, a relationship, an authority. The specific object here is crucial: **לַיהוָה** ("against the LORD"). Sodom's sin is not merely social wickedness or ethical failure; it is transgression against YHWH specifically. They are sinful *toward YHWH* — the preposition ל marks YHWH as the one against whom they offend.

**The structure:** The two terms (wicked + sinful) with the qualifier **מְאֹד** ("exceedingly/very much") at the end — placed after both terms — makes the מְאֹד modify the entire compound: they were *exceedingly* wicked *and* sinful. The extremity of the intensifier is the point: this is not ordinary wickedness. The superlative quality of Sodom's sin anticipates and justifies the superlative quality of its destruction (Genesis 19).

**The nature of Sodom's sin:** Genesis 13:13 does not specify what the wickedness consists of — only that it exists and is directed against YHWH. Genesis 18:20 speaks of the "outcry" (זַעֲקַה) of Sodom and Gomorrah as "very great" (כִּי-רַבָּה). Genesis 19 depicts violent sexual assault attempted against the angelic visitors. Ezekiel 16:49 offers a different diagnostic: "This was the guilt of your sister Sodom: she and her daughters had pride, excess of food, and prosperous ease, but did not aid the poor and needy." The EMET renders the text as it stands; the nature of the sin is left in the territory of the Hebrew narrator's deliberate generalness.

**LXX 13:13:** οἱ δὲ ἄνθρωποι οἱ ἐν Σοδόμοις πονηροὶ καὶ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ σφόδρα — "And the men in Sodom were wicked and sinful before God exceedingly." The LXX renders ל**יהוָה** as ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before God") rather than "against the LORD" — ἐναντίον can mean either "before" (in the presence of) or "against" (in opposition to). The MT's לַ (the lamed preposition with YHWH) more strongly implies direction of offense; the LXX's ἐναντίον slightly softens the directional "against" toward a spatial "in the presence of." The EMET follows the MT: "against the LORD."

---

### On 13:14 — After Lot Had Parted: The Divine Response to Human Choice

**וַיהוָה אָמַר אֶל-אַבְרָם אַחֲרֵי הִפָּרֶד-לוֹט מֵעִמּוֹ** — "And the LORD said to Abram after Lot had parted from him."

The timing of the divine speech is theologically charged: **after** (אַחֲרֵי) the separation. YHWH speaks to Abram not before the separation (to direct it) and not during Lot's choice (to intervene) but *afterward* — once Lot has departed and Abram stands alone in the land. The land that Abram just surrendered to Lot's choice (offering him first pick, v. 9), YHWH now confirms entirely to Abram and his seed.

The structure creates a contrast with Lot's mode of acquisition:
- Lot: looked with his eyes, chose by sight, took what appeared best
- Abram: deferred, let another choose, waited — and received the whole land by divine word

The **four directions** of v. 14 (north, south, east, west — צָפֹנָה וְנֶגְבָּה וְקֵדְמָה וְיָמָּה) constitute a symbolic totality: the entire compass, the whole visible land in every direction. The divine survey of the land from Abram's standpoint encompasses exactly what Lot surveyed ("Lot lifted up his eyes and saw the whole plain," v. 10), but surpasses it in scope — YHWH invites Abram to look not at one attractive sector but in every direction at once.

---

### On 13:15-16 — The Land and Seed Promises

**כִּי אֶת-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה רֹאֶה לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה וּלְזַרְעֲךָ עַד-עוֹלָם** — "For all the land that you see, to you I will give it and to your seed forever."

**The two recipients of the promise:**
1. **לְךָ** ("to you") — Abram himself receives the land in promise
2. **וּלְזַרְעֲךָ** ("and to your seed") — Abram's descendants receive it in fulfillment

**עַד-עוֹלָם ("forever/to eternity/to the age"):** עוֹלָם denotes the horizon of time, the furthest extent of temporal imagination. It does not necessarily mean infinite chronological time but "into the indefinite future, beyond the reach of current vision." The land promise is given without temporal limit or expiration — it is permanent covenant grant, not a conditional or terminable lease.

**v. 16 — כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ: "Like the dust of the earth"**

The seed-promise uses **עָפָר (afar, "dust/soil")** as the image of uncountability. This connects to two crucial earlier texts:

1. **The creation of humanity (2:7):** "YHWH God formed man from the dust of the ground (עָפָר מִן-הָאֲדָמָה)" — Adam is made from dust. Abram's seed will be *as numerous as dust* — as numerous as the very material from which humanity was made. The promise recovers and expands the Adamic commission: "be fruitful and multiply" (1:28) finds its Abrahamic fulfillment in the dust-of-the-earth multitude.

2. **The divine curse (3:19):** "for dust you are and to dust you shall return (עָפָר אַתָּה וְאֶל-עָפָר תָּשׁוּב)" — dust in the curse is the image of mortality and return to the ground. Here dust is the image of innumerability — YHWH transforms the curse-image (dust = death, finite, decomposition) into a blessing-image (dust = countless life, infinite progeny). The same Hebrew word carries both the weight of mortality and the weight of promise.

**Parallel promises:** The dust-of-the-earth image for the seed appears in Genesis 28:14 (to Jacob: "your seed shall be like the dust of the earth"). A different image — stars of heaven and sand of the seashore — appears in 15:5 (stars) and 22:17 (stars and sand). These three uncountable images (dust, stars, sand) form a coordinated set across the Abrahamic and patriarchal promises, each emphasizing from a different angle the absolute innumerability of the promised descendants.

**The conditional clause structure of v. 16** is notable: "so that if a man is able to count the dust of the earth, also your seed shall be counted" — the conditionality is an impossibility. No one can count the dust; therefore no one will count Abram's seed. The counterfactual conditional (impossible premise, impossible conclusion) is a way of saying: your seed will be *absolutely* uncountable.

**LXX 13:16:** ὡς ἡ ἄμμος τῆς γῆς — "like the sand of the earth." The LXX renders כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ as "like the *sand* of the earth" rather than "like the *dust* of the earth." The Greek word **ἄμμος** (ammos) means "sand" rather than "dust/soil." This is a significant translational shift: the LXX apparently found the dust image difficult (dust = death-association?) and replaced it with the more straightforwardly positive "sand." However, the Hebrew עָפָר is unmistakably "dust/soil" in the MT — the LXX interpretation diverges from the MT here. The EMET follows the MT: "dust of the earth."

---

### On 13:17 — הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ: Walking the Land as Possession

**קוּם הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ כִּי לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה** — "Rise up, walk about in the land, through its length and through its breadth, for to you I will give it."

**הִתְהַלֵּךְ (hithallek):** The Hithpael of הָלַךְ ("to walk") — reflexive/intensive, meaning "to walk about, traverse repeatedly." This is the same verb form used of Enoch (5:22, 24: הִתְהַלֶּךְ-חֲנוֹךְ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים, "Enoch walked with God") and will be used of God himself walking in Eden (3:8: מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּגָּן, "walking about in the garden"). It carries a sense of intimate, sustained traversal — not a single passage through but a back-and-forth, all-encompassing movement.

**Walking as a legal act of possession:** In the ancient Near East, walking the perimeter or length and breadth of a property was a recognized gesture of territorial claim and ownership. YHWH commands Abram to *walk* the land as a way of taking symbolic possession of it — his feet on the ground are a form of covenant title-deed. This is the physical/embodied counterpart to the verbal promise: YHWH speaks the promise; Abram walks the land.

**לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ ("through its length and through its breadth"):** The two dimensions (length and breadth) constitute the full extent of the land — a total traversal in both horizontal axes. This formulation anticipates later surveying language and recurs in Israelite and Jewish tradition as a way of describing comprehensive possession.

---

### On 13:18 — אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא: The Oaks of Mamre at Hebron

**וַיֶּאֱהַל אַבְרָם וַיָּבֹא וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא אֲשֶׁר בְּחֶבְרוֹן** — "And Abram pitched his tent and came and dwelt by the oaks of Mamre which are at Hebron."

**אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא ("oaks of Mamre"):** The word **אֵלוֹן** (elon, "oak" or possibly "terebinth") occurs at several sacred sites in the patriarchal narratives: the oak of Moreh at Shechem (12:6), the oaks of Mamre here, the oak at Shechem where Jacob buries the foreign gods (35:4). Sacred trees (particularly large oaks and terebinths) mark sites of divine encounter in Canaanite and Israelite religious geography — they were meeting-points between the human and divine worlds. Abram plants himself under Mamre's oaks, choosing a place already marked as sacred.

**מַמְרֵא (Mamre):** Mamre appears later as a personal name — an Amorite ally of Abram (14:13, 24). The site may be named for him or may have an older sacred name adopted by the narrative. Modern Hebron (Arabic: al-Khalīl, "the friend," a title given to Abraham) preserves the tradition: the oak of Mamre is still venerated near Hebron.

**חֶבְרוֹן (Hebron):** Hebron (modern al-Khalīl) will become one of the most significant sites in Abrahamic tradition — the location of the cave of Machpelah where Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah are all buried (Genesis 23; 25:9; 35:27-29; 49:29-32; 50:13). David's first capital before Jerusalem (2 Sam. 2:1-4). The future burial-ground of the patriarchs is first reached here, where Abram builds the third and final altar of his Canaan itinerary.

**The third altar (12:7 → 12:8 → 13:18):** Abram has now built three altars in Canaan:
1. At the oak of Moreh (Shechem), 12:7 — at the first theophany
2. Between Bethel and Ai, 12:8 — at the second encampment
3. At Mamre, Hebron, 13:18 — at the final resting point after Lot's departure

Each altar marks a divine moment: theophany, covenant worship, and now the reception of the land-and-seed promises. Abram punctuates the land with altars as he moves through it — staking the land with worship rather than with military conquest.

**The contrast with Lot, final:** The chapter closes with a precise literary contrast:
- Lot: pitched his tent near Sodom (v. 12), moving toward a city destined for destruction
- Abram: pitched his tent at Mamre (v. 18), near a place that will become the burial ground of the covenant family — a place of permanence, promise, and sacred memory

Lot chooses by sight; Abram walks by faith. Lot drifts toward Sodom; Abram settles at Mamre. Lot pitches his tent at the edge of wickedness; Abram builds an altar to the LORD.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 13

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 13:2 | כָּבֵד מְאֹד ("very heavy") | πλούσιος σφόδρα ("very wealthy") | LXX normalizes "heavy" → "wealthy"; MT's concrete idiom is lost |
| 13:5 | צֹאן וּבָקָר וְאֹהָלִים ("flocks and cattle and tents") | πρόβατα καὶ βόες καὶ σκηναί | LXX agrees with MT; standard rendering |
| 13:7 | אָז יֹשֵׁב ("were dwelling then") | τότε κατῴκει ("were dwelling then") | LXX agrees; τότε renders אָז as before |
| 13:10 | כְּגַן-יְהוָה ("like the garden of the LORD") | ὡς παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ ("like the paradise of God") | LXX = "paradise of God"; κύριος/θεός variation; meaning equivalent |
| 13:10 | כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם ("like the land of Egypt") | ὡς ἡ γῆ Αἰγύπτου ("like the land of Egypt") | LXX agrees with MT exactly |
| 13:11 | מִקֶּדֶם ("toward the east") | ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν ("from the east") | LXX agrees in direction |
| 13:13 | לַיהוָה ("against the LORD") | ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before/against God") | LXX uses θεός not κύριος; ἐναντίον slightly softer than MT's "against" |
| 13:16 | כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ ("like the dust of the earth") | ὡς ἡ ἄμμος τῆς γῆς ("like the sand of the earth") | LXX replaces "dust" with "sand" — significant divergence from MT |
| 13:17 | הִתְהַלֵּךְ ("walk about") | διόδευσον αὐτήν ("traverse it") | LXX uses a compound verb "traverse/pass through"; MT's Hithpael sustained-traversal nuance partially preserved |
| 13:18 | אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא ("oaks of Mamre") | τὴν δρῦν τὴν Μαμβρη ("the oak of Mamre") | LXX has singular "oak"; MT has plural "oaks" (אֵלֹנֵי) — minor number variant |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 13 fragments are extant from Qumran. The manifest confirms this: "DSS: No significant Genesis 13 fragments extant." The Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen) does cover the Abram-Lot narrative but with extensive rewriting rather than textual variants in the strict sense.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The SP agrees with the MT throughout Genesis 13 without significant variants.

**Targum Onkelos:** At 13:10, Onkelos renders כְּגַן-יְהוָה as "like the garden of the LORD" (following MT). The dust/seed promise (13:16) is rendered straightforwardly.

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan:** Expands v. 13 with elaboration on the sins of the Sodomites, consistent with later tradition specifying their wickedness.

**New Testament echoes:** Hebrews 11:9 alludes to Abram dwelling in the land as a sojourner in tents — the tent of 13:18 becomes an emblem of faith-as-pilgrimage in the NT interpretation. 2 Peter 2:7-8 refers to Lot as a "righteous man" (δίκαιον) tormented by the lawlessness of Sodom — the NT takes the most charitable possible view of Lot. Romans 4 and Galatians 3 develop the Abrahamic faith-by-promise theme against the background of Genesis 12-15 taken as a whole.
